analogies.schema: flux = transport_coefficient × driving_potential
analogies.mapping: electric current I ↔ radiative power flux; resistance R ↔ thermal-resistance analog; voltage V ↔ temperature difference
analogies.note: Ohm's law (V=IR) is the canonical member of the linear-transport family; same form as thermal conduction (Fourier) and diffusion (Fick).

analogies.schema: flux = (1/resistance) × gradient of potential
analogies.mapping: current density J = σE ↔ viscous momentum flux; conductivity σ ↔ viscosity μ⁻¹; potential gradient ∇V ↔ velocity gradient ∇v
analogies.note: Linear constitutive response: a flux is proportional to the gradient of a driving field via a material transport coefficient.

analogies.schema: mean occupation n(ε) = 1 / (exp(β(ε−μ)) ± 1)
analogies.mapping: +1 (fermions, exclusion) ↔ −1 (bosons, condensation); same Boltzmann factor exp(β(ε−μ))
analogies.note: Intra-physics but cross-statistics: identical functional skeleton differing only by a sign that encodes spin-statistics.

analogies.schema: continuous/discrete symmetry of a system ⟹ an invariant counted over the group
analogies.mapping: continuous symmetry of L ↔ group action on a set; conserved Noether charge ↔ number of orbits = (1/|G|)Σ|X^g|
analogies.note: Symmetry (a group) generates an invariant: a conserved charge in physics, an orbit count in combinatorics.

analogies.schema: linear operator acting on a state returns a scalar multiple: Â|v⟩ = λ|v⟩
analogies.mapping: matrix A ↔ Hamiltonian Ĥ; eigenvalue λ ↔ energy E_n; eigenvector v ↔ stationary state |n⟩
analogies.note: Quantum energy levels ARE the eigenvalue spectrum of the Hamiltonian operator — the discrete spectrum is a linear-algebra eigenproblem.

analogies.schema: characteristic equation roots = natural modes of a linear system
analogies.mapping: roots of det(A−λI)=0 ↔ poles s = roots of LC s²+RCs+1=0; eigenvalue λ ↔ resonant/decay frequency ω₀, ζ
analogies.note: Resonant modes of a circuit are eigenvalues of its state-space matrix; resonance = a near-imaginary eigenvalue.

analogies.schema: symmetric/normal operator ⟹ orthonormal eigenbasis diagonalizing it
analogies.mapping: A = QΛQᵀ ↔ covariance Σ = VΛVᵀ; eigenvectors Q ↔ principal components V; eigenvalues Λ ↔ explained variance
analogies.note: PCA is literally the spectral decomposition of a (symmetric) covariance matrix.

analogies.schema: second-order linear system: ẍ + 2ζω₀ẋ + ω₀²x = forcing
analogies.mapping: mass m ↔ inductance L; spring k ↔ 1/capacitance 1/C; damping b ↔ resistance R; position x ↔ charge q
analogies.note: The mechanical SHO and the RLC circuit obey the same ODE; the electrical–mechanical analogy is exact term-by-term.

analogies.schema: ∂²φ/∂t² = c²∇²φ  (hyperbolic 2nd-order PDE)
analogies.mapping: string displacement y ↔ electric field E; mechanical wave speed c ↔ light speed 1/√(ε₀μ₀)
analogies.note: Mechanical waves on a string and electromagnetic waves share the identical d'Alembertian operator.

analogies.schema: first-order in time, second-order in space: ∂ψ/∂t = D ∂²ψ/∂x²
analogies.mapping: diffusion constant D ↔ iℏ/2m; temperature/concentration u ↔ wave function ψ; real time ↔ imaginary time (Wick rotation)
analogies.note: The Schrödinger equation is the heat/diffusion equation with an imaginary diffusion coefficient (Wick rotation t→−iτ).

analogies.schema: transport/continuity of a density in phase space
analogies.mapping: thermal diffusion of u(x,t) ↔ collisionless flow of f(r,v,t); Laplacian spreading ↔ advection + force term (q/m)(E+v×B)·∇_v f
analogies.note: Both are conservation/transport equations for a scalar density field; heat eqn is the diffusive limit, Vlasov the collisionless limit.

analogies.schema: extremize a functional/function subject to constraints ⟹ stationarity condition
analogies.mapping: δS=0 over paths ↔ ∇f=λ∇g at a point; Euler-Lagrange equation ↔ stationarity ∇(f−λg)=0
analogies.note: Lagrangian mechanics is the calculus-of-variations / constrained-optimization framework applied to physical trajectories; both use multipliers λ.

analogies.schema: minimize an objective functional over admissible states to find the optimum
analogies.mapping: trial state |φ⟩ minimizing ⟨φ|Ĥ|φ⟩ ↔ weight w minimizing ½‖w‖²; ground-state energy E₀ ↔ max-margin solution
analogies.note: The quantum variational method and convex ML optimization both find an optimum by minimizing a (convex) functional under constraints.

analogies.schema: normalized exponential weighting: p(state) = weight / partition_sum
analogies.mapping: posterior P(A|B)=P(B|A)P(A)/P(B) ↔ Boltzmann p_i = e^{−βE_i}/Z; evidence P(B) ↔ partition function Z
analogies.note: The partition function Z is the statistical-mechanics analog of the Bayesian evidence (normalizing constant); both are sums over states.

analogies.schema: −Σ p log p as an information measure of uncertainty/spread
analogies.mapping: Gibbs entropy S=−k_B Σ p_i ln p_i ↔ Shannon entropy / cross-entropy loss; thermal disorder ↔ predictive uncertainty
analogies.note: Statistical-mechanics entropy and information/cross-entropy in ML are the same Shannon functional −Σp log p.

analogies.schema: dN/dt = −kN  ⟹  N(t) = N₀ e^{−kt}
analogies.mapping: decay constant λ ↔ rate constant k; nuclei N ↔ concentration [A]; half-life ln2/λ ↔ ln2/k
analogies.note: Radioactive decay and first-order chemical kinetics are the same first-order linear ODE; both have a constant half-life.

analogies.schema: exponential relaxation toward equilibrium with time constant τ
analogies.mapping: N(t)=N₀e^{−λt} ↔ V_C(t)=V_f+(V_0−V_f)e^{−t/RC}; mean-life 1/λ ↔ τ=RC
analogies.note: Nuclear decay, capacitor discharge, and first-order kinetics are all single-exponential relaxation processes.

analogies.schema: equilibrium where forward and reverse (supply and demand) rates balance
analogies.mapping: K_c balancing forward/reverse reaction ↔ P* where Q_d(P)=Q_s(P); Le Chatelier shift ↔ comparative statics of a demand/supply shock
analogies.note: Chemical equilibrium and market equilibrium share the fixed-point structure: a balance of opposing flows, with shocks producing comparative-statics shifts.

analogies.schema: potential = reference + (kT) × log(activity ratio)
analogies.mapping: pH = pK_a + log([A⁻]/[HA]) ↔ μ = μ° + k_BT ln(activity); log-of-concentration-ratio drives the potential
analogies.note: Buffer pH (Henderson-Hasselbalch) and the chemical/Nernst potential share the 'potential = const + log(ratio)' Boltzmann form.

analogies.schema: conjugate variables linked by a Fourier transform obey Δx·Δk ≥ const
analogies.mapping: signal time–frequency bandwidth product ↔ σ_x·σ_p ≥ ℏ/2; narrow in x ↔ broad in k
analogies.note: The Heisenberg uncertainty principle is the Fourier bandwidth theorem: a function and its transform cannot both be sharply localized.

analogies.schema: convolution in one domain = pointwise product in the Fourier-conjugate domain
analogies.mapping: F{x*h}=X·H (signals) ↔ FT[f*g]=f̃·g̃ (math methods); LTI filtering ↔ Green's-function response
analogies.note: The signals-and-systems convolution theorem and the physics Fourier convolution theorem are the identical statement.

analogies.schema: conservation at a junction: net flux in = net flux out
analogies.mapping: A₁v₁=A₂v₂ (mass flux) ↔ ΣI_in=ΣI_out (charge flux); incompressible flow ↔ charge conservation at a node
analogies.note: Fluid continuity and Kirchhoff's current law are both local conservation (divergence-free flux) statements.

analogies.schema: d(stock)/dt = inflow − outflow
analogies.mapping: Δk = s·f(k) − (δ+n+g)k ↔ dU = δQ − δW; investment ↔ heat in; depreciation ↔ work out
analogies.note: The Solow capital-accumulation equation and the first law are both stock-flow balance equations: rate of change of a stock equals net flow.

analogies.schema: saturating response: y = y_max · x / (K + x) form bounded above
analogies.mapping: v = V_max[S]/(K_M+[S]) ↔ occupation saturating to 1; substrate [S] ↔ energy/chemical-potential offset; K_M ↔ kT scale
analogies.note: Enzyme saturation kinetics and Fermi occupation are both monotone saturating (sigmoidal/hyperbolic) response curves bounded by a maximum.
